title_en: Zakat and Fasting in Bada'i as-Sana'i
content_en:
Al-Kasani's treatment of zakat in Bada'i as-Sana'i follows his systematic organization: definition, types, conditions, elements, calculation, distribution, and invalidators. This comprehensive framework makes it easy to understand both the full scope of zakat obligations and the reasoning behind each aspect.

Al-Kasani defines zakat as 'making a portion of specified wealth, reaching the nisab after the completion of a year, the property of a specified poor person for the sake of Allah.' Each element of this definition corresponds to a legal condition: 'specified wealth' restricts it to the zakatable categories; 'reaching the nisab' sets the threshold; 'after completion of a year' establishes the hawl requirement; 'property of a poor person' defines the distribution requirement; and 'for the sake of Allah' establishes the intention requirement.

The Hanafi school's treatment of business partnership assets and their zakat is presented with al-Kasani's characteristic clarity. In a commercial partnership, each partner is responsible for zakat on their own share of the partnership assets. If the partnership has incurred debts, the Hanafi school permits deducting the value of debts from zakatable assets before calculating zakat — unlike the Shafi'i school which does not permit this deduction. Al-Kasani defends the debt deduction principle with the argument that only net wealth truly belongs to the owner; debt-encumbered assets are partially the creditor's property.

The chapter on agricultural zakat presents the Hanafi school's broader scope: zakat applies to all agricultural produce that comes from the land, not only to grains, dates, and raisins as the other schools limit it. Al-Kasani grounds this in the hadith 'On that which the sky waters or springs water, a tenth is due' — which he interprets as applying to all irrigated produce rather than only specific commodities. This broader scope, unique to the Hanafi school, means that vegetables, fruits, and other crops are also subject to zakat in the Hanafi tradition.

For fasting, al-Kasani's systematic approach organizes the discussion around: the fasting obligation, its types (obligatory, recommended, disliked, forbidden), its conditions, its elements (intention and abstaining from nullifiers), its nullifiers, and its consequences (qada' and kaffarah). This organization ensures that the student understands not just what breaks the fast but what legal consequence follows each type of break.

The Hanafi school's unique position on certain fast nullifiers is presented with al-Kasani's care for the evidential basis. The position that reaching a state of junub (major ritual impurity) during the day — not through voluntary sexual activity but through involuntary emission during sleep — does not break the fast is affirmed. The position that cupping (hijamah) does not break the fast in the Hanafi school (contrary to the Hanbali view based on a hadith the Hanafi school considers abrogated) is defended with reference to later hadiths showing the Prophet and Companions observing the fast while cupping.
title_id: Zakat dan Puasa dalam Bada'i as-Sana'i
content_id:
Perlakuan al-Kasani terhadap zakat dalam Bada'i as-Sana'i mengikuti susunan sistematisnya: definisi, jenis-jenis, syarat-syarat, unsur-unsur, perhitungan, distribusi, dan pembatal. Kerangka komprehensif ini menjadikan bab zakat sebagai salah satu yang paling berguna dari Bada'i untuk siswa yang perlu memahami hukum zakat secara sistematis. Definisi zakat al-Kasani — sebagai kewajiban harta yang ditetapkan oleh hukum ilahi atas harta-harta tertentu untuk orang-orang tertentu — menetapkan dua parameter yang kemudian ia analisis secara rinci: harta mana yang tunduk pada kewajiban, dan penerima mana yang berhak menerima zakat.

Syarat-syarat kewajiban zakat — kepemilikan nisab, haul (berputarnya satu tahun penuh atas nisab), kepemilikan penuh, dan kebebasan dari hutang yang mengurangi nisab di bawah ambang batas — disajikan oleh al-Kasani dengan perhatian pada kasus-kasus batas dan situasi-situasi yang meragukan di mana kondisi-kondisi dapat tidak terpenuhi. Analisis ini sangat berguna untuk pertanyaan-pertanyaan hukum praktis tentang kewajiban zakat seseorang.

Perlakuan terhadap puasa menerapkan kerangka yang sama pada kewajiban Ramadan dan puasa sunnah. Kondisi-kondisi yang membatalkan puasa disajikan dengan ketepatan yang khas: al-Kasani membedakan antara apa yang secara esensial membatalkan puasa (qadha diperlukan), apa yang membatalkan puasa dan memerlukan kafarat juga, dan apa yang hanya makruh tanpa membatalkan puasa. Hierarki hukum ini sangat penting bagi pelajar dan disajikan dengan kejelasan yang menjadi ciri khas pendekatan al-Kasani dalam seluruh Bada'i as-Sana'i.
content_ar: Al-Kasani's treatment of الزكاة in Bada'i as-Sana'i follows his systematic organization: definition, types, conditions, elements, calculation, distribution, and invalidators. This comprehensive framework makes it easy لفهم both the full scope of الزكاة obligations and the reasoning behind each aspect. Al-Kasani defines الزكاة as 'making a portion of specified الثروة, reaching the nisab بعد the completion of a year, the property of a specified poor person for the sake of الله.' Each element of this definition corresponds to a legal condition: 'specified الثروة' restricts it to the zakatable categories; 'reaching the nisab' sets the threshold; 'بعد completion of a year' establishes the hawl requirement; 'property of a poor person' defines the distribution requirement; and 'for the sake of الله' establishes the intention requirement. The الحنفي school's treatment of business partnership assets and their الزكاة is presented with al-Kasani's characteristic clarity. In a commercial partnership, each partner is responsible for الزكاة on their own share of the partnership assets. إذا the partnership has incurred debts, the الحنفي school permits deducting the value of debts from zakatable assets قبل calculating الزكاة — unlike the الشافعي school which does not permit this deduction. Al-Kasani defends the debt deduction principle with the argument that only net الثروة truly belongs to the owner; debt-encumbered assets are partially the creditor's property. The الباب on agricultural الزكاة presents the الحنفي school's broader scope: الزكاة applies to all agricultural produce that comes from the land, not only to grains, dates, and raisins as the other schools limit it. Al-Kasani grounds this in the الحديث 'On that which the sky waters or springs water, a عاشراً is due' — which he interprets as applying to all irrigated produce rather than only specific commodities. This broader scope, unique to the الحنفي school, means that vegetables, fruits, and other crops are أيضاً subject to الزكاة in the الحنفي التراث. For الصيام, al-Kasani's systematic approach organizes the discussion around: the الصيام الالتزام, its types (obligatory, recommended, disliked, forbidden), its conditions, its elements (intention and abstaining from nullifiers), its nullifiers, and its consequences (القضاء' and kaffarah). This organization ensures that the student understands not just what breaks the fast but what legal consequence follows each type of break. The الحنفي school's unique position on certain fast nullifiers is presented with al-Kasani's care for the evidential basis. The position that reaching a state of junub (major ritual impurity) خلال the day — not through voluntary sexual activity but through involuntary emission خلال sleep — does not break the fast is affirmed.
